---
title: "BNR Information Hub Statistical Analysis Plan"
subtitle: "Standing analytical methods for BNR metrics and published analytical outputs"
date: "2026-05-15"
version: "Draft v0.1"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
---

# BNR Information Hub Statistical Analysis Plan

## Document control

| Item | Detail |
|---|---|
| Document name | BNR Information Hub Statistical Analysis Plan |
| Short name | Info-hub SAP |
| Version | Draft v0.1 |
| Date | 15 May 2026 |
| Owner | BNR Analytics Team |
| Companion document | [BNR Metric Specification Register](sap-metrics.qmd) |

## Purpose

This Statistical Analysis Plan defines the standing analytical methods used to produce BNR information hub metrics and published analytical outputs.

The metric system is the permanent BNR deliverable. Briefings are temporary interpretations that consume those metrics. A briefing may go beyond the defined core metrics where this supports a specific research, interpretation or communication purpose.

This SAP exists to ensure that BNR outputs are reproducible, auditable, interpretable and consistent across data releases.

## Scope

This SAP applies to:

- routine surveillance metrics
- data quality metrics
- process monitoring metrics
- public dashboards
- static briefings
- downloadable analytical datasets
- tables and figures derived from approved BNR data releases

Individual metric definitions are maintained in the companion [BNR Metric Specification Register](sap-metrics.qmd).

## Core principles

All BNR information hub analytics follow these principles:

1. Metrics are defined before routine production.
2. Approved metric definitions are stable across data releases.
3. Stratifications are dimensions, not separate metrics, unless the definition or method changes.
4. Stata is the primary analytical engine for core surveillance computation.
5. Quarto is used to publish and present approved outputs, not to define core analytical logic.
6. Outputs are deterministic and reproducible from approved data releases and versioned code.
7. Metadata is part of the analytical product.
8. Briefings may interpret, combine or extend metrics, but do not redefine core metrics.

## Data release model

Routine metrics are calculated from approved BNR data releases.

Each analytical cycle uses a defined data freeze. If the underlying data are corrected or updated after that freeze, the change is handled as a new analytical cycle.

Each metric run should record, at minimum:

- data release identifier
- source dataset version
- run date
- responsible Stata DO or ADO file
- data status, such as final or provisional

## Units of analysis

BNR metrics may use different units of analysis. The unit must be explicit in the [BNR Metric Specification Register](sap-metrics.qmd).

| Unit | Definition |
|---|---|
| Event | A registered CVD event, such as stroke or acute myocardial infarction |
| Person | A unique individual in the registry or linked source system |
| Admission | A hospital admission associated with a registered event |
| Death | A death record or death outcome associated with a registered event |
| Bed-day | One occupied hospital bed for one day |
| Population | Resident population denominator used for rate calculation |
| Record | A row in a registry or release dataset, usually used for data quality metrics |

The same individual may contribute more than one event where the metric is event-based.

## Standard CVD data sources

Current CVD metrics may use:

| Source | Use |
|---|---|
| BNR CVD registry data | Case ascertainment, event characteristics, admissions and outcomes |
| Death certificate data | Death certificate only cases and death outcomes where used |
| Population denominator data | Crude and age-standardised rate calculation |
| Data release metadata | Dataset versioning, release status and provenance |

Additional sources may be added for future surveillance domains.

## Standard inclusion rules

Unless a metric-specific definition states otherwise, CVD metrics include:

- confirmed BNR events
- Barbados residents
- events occurring within the defined reporting period
- records included in the approved analytical data release
- hospital-registered stroke and acute myocardial infarction events for current CVD reporting

The [BNR Metric Specification Register](sap-metrics.qmd) must define whether a metric includes all events, first-ever events, hospitalised events, death certificate only events, or another specified event set.

## Standard exclusion rules

Unless a metric-specific definition states otherwise, CVD metrics exclude:

- non-residents
- duplicate records
- records outside the reporting period
- records failing the approved case definition
- records with missing required values for the metric being calculated

Death certificate only cases are included or excluded according to the metric definition. They must not be silently removed.

## Standard dimensions

Dimensions are the approved ways a metric may be stratified. Stratification does not create a new metric unless the definition or method changes.

Standard CVD dimensions include:

| Dimension | Example values |
|---|---|
| Period | Year, quarter, month, week, rolling period |
| Event type | All CVD, stroke, acute myocardial infarction |
| Sex | Female, male, all |
| Age group | All ages, standard BNR age groups |
| Source status | Hospital-registered, death certificate only, all sources |
| Outcome status | Survived to discharge, died in hospital, unknown |
| Data release | Current release, historical release |

A metric does not need to use every dimension.

## Period definitions

Calendar year is the standard annual reporting period.

Monthly and weekly outputs may be produced for operational monitoring. Weekly metrics should use one documented week definition consistently.

Rolling periods may be used for smoothing, baseline comparison or small-number stability. The number of years in the rolling period must be recorded in the metric output.

## Statistical methods

### Count metrics

Count metrics report the number of eligible units in a defined period and stratum.

$$
\text{Count} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} I_i
$$

where \(I_i = 1\) when record \(i\) meets the metric definition, and \(I_i = 0\) otherwise.

Counts should normally be reported alongside relevant denominators, percentages or rates when used for interpretation.

### Percentage metrics

Percentage metrics report a numerator as a share of an eligible denominator.

$$
\text{Percentage} = \frac{x}{n} \times 100
$$

where \(x\) is the number meeting the metric condition and \(n\) is the eligible denominator.

Where appropriate, percentage metrics should include 95% confidence intervals using a documented binomial method.

### Rate metrics

Rate metrics report the number of events relative to a population denominator.

$$
\text{Rate per 100,000} = \frac{x}{N} \times 100{,}000
$$

where \(x\) is the number of eligible events and \(N\) is the resident population denominator.

The denominator source, age bands and reference period must be documented.

### Age-standardised rates

Age-standardised rates are used when comparing populations or time periods with different age structures.

Direct age-standardisation is the default method unless a metric-specific definition states otherwise.

$$
\text{Age-standardised rate} = \sum_{a=1}^{A} w_a r_a
$$

where \(r_a\) is the age-specific rate in age group \(a\), and \(w_a\) is the corresponding standard population weight.

The [BNR Metric Specification Register](metric-specification-register.qmd) must define the age bands, denominator source and standard population.

### Ratio metrics

Ratio metrics compare two rates, percentages or counts.

$$
\text{Ratio} = \frac{\text{comparison estimate}}{\text{reference estimate}}
$$

The comparison group and reference group must be explicit.

Ratio metrics should only be added to the register when they are expected to be routinely produced and interpreted. Ad hoc ratios may appear in briefings as research outputs.

### Model-adjusted metrics

Model-adjusted metrics may be used where crude stratification is insufficient.

Examples include:

- age-adjusted case-fatality percentage
- model-based rate ratio
- trend estimate

The model form, adjustment variables, reference group and prediction method must be documented in the [BNR Metric Specification Register](sap-metrics.qmd).

Model-adjusted metrics should be used sparingly and only when they add clear interpretive value.

### Length-of-stay metrics

Length of stay is calculated from admission and discharge dates.

$$
\text{Length of stay}_i = \text{date of discharge}_i - \text{date of admission}_i
$$

The metric definition must specify handling of:

- same-day discharge
- in-hospital death
- missing dates
- implausible date sequences

Median and interquartile range are preferred for public reporting because length of stay is usually skewed.

### Bed-day metrics

Bed-day metrics describe hospital service use.

$$
\text{Total bed-days} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{length of stay}_i
$$

Bed-day metrics should be interpreted as service-use measures, not disease incidence measures.

## Data quality metrics

Data quality metrics are part of the routine metric system.

They may describe:

- release size
- missingness
- duplicate candidates
- invalid dates
- implausible values
- unknown outcomes
- abstraction lag
- release readiness

Data quality metrics may be internal only, public, or partially public depending on governance decisions.

## Uncertainty intervals

Uncertainty intervals should be produced when they support interpretation.

| Metric type | Default uncertainty approach |
|---|---|
| Count | Usually none, unless modelled or compared |
| Percentage | Binomial confidence interval |
| Crude rate | Poisson or exact Poisson confidence interval |
| Age-standardised rate | Standard direct standardisation confidence interval |
| Model-adjusted estimate | Model-based confidence interval |
| Median | Optional bootstrap or distribution-free interval, where needed |

The method used must be recorded in the output metadata or metric register.

## Small numbers and disclosure control

All public outputs must comply with agreed disclosure control rules.

Metric values may be flagged as:

- final
- provisional
- suppressed
- low count
- unstable
- data quality warning
- internal only

Suppression rules should be implemented consistently in Stata wherever possible, not manually in Quarto.

Where values are not suppressed but are unstable, public interpretation should avoid overstatement.

## Missing data

The handling of missing data must be explicit.

Default rules are:

- missing values in required numerator fields exclude a record from that metric
- missing denominator fields exclude a record from denominator-based metrics
- missing outcome values exclude records from outcome metrics unless unknown is a valid category
- missingness should be reported as a data quality metric where it affects interpretation

Imputation is not used for routine public metrics unless a metric-specific definition explicitly approves it.

## Baseline comparisons

Baseline comparisons may use historical reference periods such as a five-year average.

The metric definition must state:

- baseline years
- inclusion rules for baseline data
- whether the baseline is fixed or rolling
- whether baseline values are means, medians, ranges or intervals
- whether the comparison is descriptive or inferential

Baseline metrics should not be confused with forecast or expected-value models unless such models are explicitly defined.

## Output requirements

Routine metric outputs should be written in long format wherever possible.

The minimum preferred output structure is:

| Field | Purpose |
|---|---|
| metric_id | Links the output row to the metric register |
| release_id | Identifies the data release |
| period | Reporting period |
| strata | Compact description of the stratum, or separate dimension variables if easier |
| statistic | Count, rate, percentage, median, mean or other statistic |
| value | Main estimate |
| unit | Count, percent, per 100,000, days, bed-days or ratio |
| numerator | Numerator, where applicable |
| denominator | Denominator, where applicable |
| lower_ci | Lower confidence interval, where applicable |
| upper_ci | Upper confidence interval, where applicable |
| status_flag | Final, provisional, suppressed, low count, unstable or warning status |

Other metadata should be stored once at dataset or build level rather than repeated unnecessarily in every row.

## Metadata requirements

Every routine metric output must include metadata describing:

- metric definition
- numerator
- denominator
- inclusion and exclusion rules
- source dataset
- data release
- calculation method
- standardisation method, where applicable
- dimensions included
- limitations
- responsible code file
- run date

Where possible, metadata should be stored both in the Stata dataset and as a companion YAML file.

## Quality assurance

Each analytical cycle should include:

- confirmation of approved data release
- validation of required variables
- dataset signature or release identifier check
- comparison with previous release totals
- review of data quality flags
- review of suppressed or unstable outputs
- Stata log review
- human sign-off before public publication

Any material difference from the approved metric definition must be documented before publication.

## Publication rules

The information hub publishes static outputs.

Quarto pages may:

- read approved metric outputs
- present metric values
- visualise approved data
- provide interpretation
- link to downloads

Quarto pages must not silently redefine numerators, denominators, exclusions or statistical methods.

## Briefings

Briefings are narrative or research products.

A briefing may:

- use approved core metrics
- combine several metrics
- visualise selected metrics
- interpret observed patterns
- include additional bespoke analysis
- explore research questions not covered by the metric register

Where a briefing includes analysis outside the metric register, that analysis should be documented within the briefing materials and should not be treated as a new core metric unless it is later approved and added to the register.

## Change control

Changes to this SAP are required when:

- a core analytical method changes
- the data release model changes
- disclosure rules change
- standard dimensions change
- a new surveillance domain introduces methods not covered here
- output metadata requirements change

Changes to individual metric definitions are recorded in the [BNR Metric Specification Register](sap-metrics.qmd).

## Version history

| Version | Date | Summary | Author |
|---|---|---|---|
| Draft v0.1 | 2026-05-15 | Initial draft SAP for the BNR information hub metric system |
